one_line_takeaway: VLSafetyBencher automates LVLM safety-benchmark construction end-to-end via four agents (preprocess, generate, augment, criterion-based select) and, on the paper's own metrics, produces benchmarks with roughly double the discriminative power (MAD) and greater score spread (GAP) than manual (SafeBench, MLLMGuard) and prior automated (AutoBencher, DataGen, DME) baselines, at $1.34 total API cost and about one week of construction time.
key_finding: On Table 1 (benchmark-quality comparison, %), VLSafetyBencher achieves MAD=15.03, MEAN=39.16, GAP=69.97, DIV=83.10, versus baselines SafeBench (MAD=8.32, MEAN=22.81, GAP=54.30, DIV=82.05), MLLMGuard (6.73, 34.32, 46.38, 82.64), DataGen (7.18, 29.11, 49.88, 77.66), AutoBencher (7.63, 39.32, 50.33, 78.99), and DME+MLLMGuard (7.10, 33.84, 47.80, 82.98) -- roughly double the MAD of prior methods and a 15.67-point GAP improvement over the next-best; AutoBencher slightly exceeds VLSafetyBencher on MEAN (harmfulness), which the authors attribute to AutoBencher directly optimizing for ASR rather than discriminability. Construction took about 1 week (7.57 days per the ablation table) versus 5.6 days for the authors' AutoBencher reproduction and months for manual benchmarks, at a reported total API cost of $1.34. In the benchmark-updating experiment (Table 2), replacing the worst 50% of samples in SafeBench raised MAD from 8.32 to 10.58 and GAP from 54.30 to 59.67; replacing 50% of MLLMGuard raised MAD from 6.73 to 11.19 and GAP from 46.38 to 56.66 (20% updates on MLLMGuard showed a slight harmfulness dip, attributed to that dataset's originally high but low-discriminative-power harmfulness). On the resulting benchmark, evaluating 20 of the 35 LVLMs (Table 3) yielded safety rates spanning 27.81% (InstructBLIP-Vicuna-7B) to 97.78% (Claude-Sonnet-4), with proprietary models (GPT-4o, GPT-4.1, Claude-Sonnet-4, Gemini-2.5-Pro) clustering at 78-98% safety and smaller/language-modeling-oriented open models (Ovis2, SAIL-VL, Pixtral, InstructBLIP) at roughly 30-60%. Ablation (Table 4) showed removing the Selection agent caused the largest quality drop (MAD 15.03->5.18, GAP 69.97->40.37) while cutting time to 3.12 days; removing Preprocessing barely changed quality (MAD 15.01) but pushed time to >=14 days; removing Augmentation most reduced harmfulness/MEAN (39.16->30.15).
core_contribution: The paper introduces VLSafetyBencher, described as the first fully automated multi-agent pipeline for constructing (and updating) safety evaluation benchmarks for large vision-language models (LVLMs), without human annotation. It comprises four serialized agents: (1) a Data Preprocessing agent that deduplicates/filters a ~300K-image raw pool (134K from existing safety datasets, 20K general images, 40K diffusion-generated, 106K scraped from social media) and categorizes samples into a 6-category/20-subcategory safety taxonomy (Privacy, Bias, Toxicity, Legality, Misinformation, Health Risk); (2) a Data Generation agent that synthesizes cross-modal image-question pairs using three interaction strategies -- Modality Dependence, Modality Complementarity, and Modality Conflict -- designed so neither the image nor the text alone reveals the harmful intent, forcing genuine cross-modal reasoning; (3) an Augmentation agent that applies text mutation (role-play/context tricks, paraphrasing, jailbreak techniques) and image mutation (cropping, blurring, noise, spatial transforms, inserted objects) to increase diversity/harmfulness; and (4) a Selection agent that formalizes benchmark quality as an optimization problem over three desiderata -- Separability (entropy of harmful-response probability across models), Harmfulness (mean judge-assessed harm probability), and Diversity (category-entropy gain plus CLIP-embedding cosine distance) -- combined via weighted sum (alpha=0.5, beta=0.3, gamma=0.2, chosen by grid search) and solved with a greedy iterative selection algorithm (Algorithm 1) that the authors argue reaches the problem's global optimum given the additive/greedy structure.
threat_model: Not an adversarial attack/defense paper in the classic sense; the 'threat model' is the space of unsafe LVLM behaviors the generated benchmark is meant to elicit (toxic responses, biased arguments, privacy leakage, health-risk/legality/misinformation content) via crafted image-question pairs, including jailbreak-augmented prompts. The paper does not model an external attacker breaching the pipeline itself; the agents and data-generation process (which uses jailbreaking techniques such as role-play tricks and cipher-style prompts) run inside Docker containers 'to ensure safety,' but no adversary against the pipeline/agents is analyzed.
limitations: The paper's own text flags several caveats rather than presenting these as external critique: (1) the 'global optimum' claim for the Selection agent's greedy Algorithm 1 rests on treating Csep, Charm, Cdiv as independent positive per-sample scores, an approximation the authors themselves note ('for simplicity, we assume...'); Cdiv is explicitly sequence-dependent (depends on already-selected DS), so the claimed global optimality is not rigorously established for that term. (2) Weight selection (alpha, beta, gamma) is via grid search on the same benchmark used for evaluation, raising a risk of circularity between benchmark construction and benchmark quality metrics (MAD/GAP are partly functions of the same separability/harmfulness criteria being optimized). (3) Human-consistency validation of the three cross-modal strategies used only 150 manually annotated queries (50 per strategy), yielding 93.5% consistency -- a fairly small manual check for a fully automated 4000-sample benchmark. (4) AutoBencher outperforms VLSafetyBencher on the MEAN (harmfulness) metric, and the paper's given explanation (different optimization objective) is not independently verified. (5) The paper reports only monetary/API cost ($1.34) for construction, without discussing compute cost of the 16 A800 GPUs used throughout. (6) No comparison to non-reproduced published results for AutoBencher/DataGen/DME on multimodal safety exists, since baselines were the authors' own reproductions adapted to the multimodal setting.

datasets_used: Raw data pool of ~300K images (134K from existing safety datasets, 20K general images, 40K diffusion-generated, 106K scraped from social media)
VLSBench (referenced as a manually annotated dataset for cost comparison)
quantitative_results: Construction time: ~7.57 days (full pipeline) vs 5.6 days for authors' AutoBencher reproduction vs months for manual benchmarks
Table 2 (SafeBench update): original MAD=8.32/MEAN=22.81/GAP=54.30; 20% update -> 8.45/26.88/56.77; 50% update -> 10.58/31.76/59.67
Table 2 (MLLMGuard update): original MAD=6.73/MEAN=34.32/GAP=46.38; 20% update -> 9.36/32.39/47.06; 50% update -> 11.19/35.78/56.66

Table 3 (20 of 35 evaluated LVLMs): overall safety rate range 27.81% (InstructBLIP-Vicuna-7B) to 97.78% (Claude-Sonnet-4); e.g. GPT-4o ASR=6.22%/SR=93.78%, GPT-4.1 ASR=7.13%/SR=92.87%, Gemini-2.5-Pro ASR=21.41%/SR=78.59%, Ovis2-8B ASR=44.85%/SR=55.15%, SAIL-VL-8B ASR=51.70%/SR=48.30%

Table 4 ablation: w/o Preprocessing MAD=15.01/MEAN=38.96/GAP=68.53/DIV=83.14, time>=14d; w/o Generation MAD=12.94/MEAN=33.28/GAP=64.37/DIV=82.66, time=4.80d; w/o Augmentation MAD=11.73/MEAN=30.15/GAP=61.61/DIV=82.81, time=6.82d; w/o Selection MAD=5.18/MEAN=20.76/GAP=40.37/DIV=82.76, time=3.12d; full system MAD=15.03/MEAN=39.16/GAP=69.97/DIV=83.10, time=7.57d
Human-consistency check on cross-modal generation strategies: 140/150 manually annotated samples matched intended strategy (93.5% consistency)
Benchmark taxonomy composition (Figure 4): Health Risk 28.7%, Toxicity 23.3%, Legality 14.6%, Misinformation 12.3%, Privacy 10.6%, Bias 10.5%
Strategy proportions in final benchmark (Figure 3b): Modality Dependency 46%, Modality Complementarity 33%, Modality Conflict 21%
baselines_compared: AutoBencher (automated, reproduced by authors for the multimodal setting)
DME (Dynamic Multimodal Evaluation, applied as DME+MLLMGuard update baseline)
